sections.heading: Calibration Overview
sections.content: NEXUS requires two independent calibration steps before the targeting system can operate with full accuracy. Step 1 — Stereo Calibration maps the geometric relationship between both cameras so depth can be computed via triangulation. Step 2 — Laser Alignment maps 2D pixel coordinates in the camera frame to physical servo pan/tilt angles. Both calibrations are performed through dedicated tabs in the NEXUS interface and their results are saved as .npz files in the data/ folder.

sections.heading: Calibration File Reference
rows: Camera matrices, distortion coeffs, R, T, rectification maps
Same as above but MATLAB-precision values

sections.heading: Step 1: Stereo Camera Calibration
sections.content: Stereo calibration computes the intrinsic parameters of each camera (focal length, principal point, distortion coefficients) and the extrinsic relationship between them (rotation matrix R, translation vector T). These are used to compute rectification maps that align both camera images to a common epipolar plane, enabling disparity computation.
steps: Print a 9×6 inner-corner chessboard pattern on rigid flat paper — A3 size is recommended. Ensure it lies perfectly flat (mount on cardboard or foam board)
Go to the 🔬 PY CALIBRATION tab
Set Square Size (mm) — measure one printed square precisely with calipers. This value directly affects depth accuracy
Click START CAPTURE — both camera feeds appear side by side
Move the chessboard to 20+ varied positions: different angles (tilted, rotated), different distances (0.5m to 2m), all four corners of the frame, and different orientations (portrait and landscape)
Each valid capture shows a green overlay on detected corners — click CAPTURE PAIR or press Space
Once you have 20+ pairs, click RUN CALIBRATION
The calibration worker runs cv2.stereoCalibrate() in a background thread — progress shown in the log
Review the RMS Reprojection Error — target < 1.0 px
Click SAVE CALIBRATION to write data/stereo_calibration_py.npz

sections.heading: Calibration Quality Metrics
rows: Must recalibrate — check lighting, board flatness, and image sharpness

sections.heading: Chessboard Capture Best Practices
sections.content: The quality of calibration images is the single most important factor for low RMS error. Follow these guidelines:
list: Coverage: Fill all regions of the frame — top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right, and center. Bad coverage causes high distortion errors at frame edges
Angles: Tilt the board 20–45° in X and Y axes. Images where the board is perfectly parallel to the camera plane contribute little to calibration
Distance: Capture at 3+ different distances. Minimum ~40 cm, maximum ~200 cm
Sharpness: Ensure both cameras are in focus. Blurry images cause incorrect corner detections
Lighting: Avoid glare and shadows on the chessboard. Even diffuse lighting is ideal
Synchrony: Both cameras must capture the board at the same moment — the board must be completely still during capture

sections.heading: What Gets Saved (stereo_calibration_py.npz)
sections.code:
# Contents of stereo_calibration_py.npz
np.load('data/stereo_calibration_py.npz', allow_pickle=True)

# Keys in the archive:
# 'K1'          : (3,3) — Left camera intrinsic matrix
# 'D1'          : (1,5) — Left camera distortion coefficients
# 'K2'          : (3,3) — Right camera intrinsic matrix
# 'D2'          : (1,5) — Right camera distortion coefficients
# 'R'           : (3,3) — Rotation matrix between cameras
# 'T'           : (3,1) — Translation vector between cameras (baseline)
# 'E'           : (3,3) — Essential matrix
# 'F'           : (3,3) — Fundamental matrix
# 'mapL1','mapL2' : Left rectification maps (for cv2.remap)
# 'mapR1','mapR2' : Right rectification maps (for cv2.remap)
# 'focal_px'    : float — Focal length in pixels (after rectification)
# 'baseline_cm' : float — Camera separation in cm (from |T|)
# 'rms'         : float — Final RMS reprojection error

sections.heading: How the Engine Uses Stereo Calibration
sections.code:
# In vision_engine.py — setup_matrices()
data = np.load(CONFIG['STEREO_FILE'])
self.mapL1   = data['mapL1']
self.mapL2   = data['mapL2']
self.mapR1   = data['mapR1']
self.mapR2   = data['mapR2']
self.focal_px    = float(data['focal_px'])
self.baseline_cm = float(data['baseline_cm'])

# In run() — applied each frame:
left_rect  = cv2.remap(left_frame,  self.mapL1, self.mapL2, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)
right_rect = cv2.remap(right_frame, self.mapR1, self.mapR2, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)
# Now left_rect and right_rect are epipolar-aligned

sections.heading: Step 2: Laser Alignment (Homography Calibration)
sections.content: Laser alignment creates a homography matrix H that maps any pixel coordinate (cx, cy) in the left camera frame to physical servo angles (pan, tilt). This is done by collecting N≥12 correspondence pairs: click on a visible laser dot in the camera feed, then the system records both the pixel position and the current servo angles reported by the ESP32.
steps: Go to the 🔵 LASER ALIGNMENT tab
Ensure ESP32 is connected (green HW status) and laser is powered ON
Click START PREVIEW to see the live camera feed
Manually aim the laser to a point using the Manual Pan/Tilt sliders in the Hardware Control tab, or by clicking on the video feed
When the laser dot is clearly visible, click on it in the camera feed — this records the pixel coordinates (cx, cy)
The current servo angles (pan, tilt) are automatically read from the hardware link and stored as the corresponding angle pair
Move the laser to a new position and repeat — aim for coverage across the full field of view: corners, edges, and center
Collect at least 12 correspondence points (20+ recommended for higher accuracy)
Click COMPUTE ALIGNMENT — this runs cv2.findHomography() with RANSAC
The matrix is saved to data/laser_alignment.npz — reload the engine to apply it

sections.heading: What Gets Saved (laser_alignment.npz)
sections.code:
# Contents of laser_alignment.npz
np.load('data/laser_alignment.npz', allow_pickle=True)

# Keys:
# 'H'           : (3,3) float64 — Homography matrix (pixel → servo angles)
# 'src_points'  : (N,2) — Pixel coordinates used to compute H
# 'dst_points'  : (N,2) — Servo angle pairs corresponding to src_points
# 'point_count' : int   — Number of correspondence points used
# 'reprojection_error' : float — Mean pixel reprojection error of H

# Usage in vision_engine.py:
data     = np.load('data/laser_alignment.npz')
self.H   = data['H']  # The 3×3 homography matrix

# Apply during inference:
# pan, tilt = CalibrationMath.apply_homography(cx, cy, self.H)

sections.heading: Homography Computation (CalibrationMath)
sections.code:
# From core/math_calib.py
@staticmethod
def calculate_homography(src: np.ndarray, dst: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Compute homography from N pixel→angle correspondences.
    Uses RANSAC for outlier rejection.
    src: (N,2) pixel coordinates  [cx, cy]
    dst: (N,2) servo angles       [pan, tilt]
    Returns: H (3,3) homography matrix
    """
    H, mask = cv2.findHomography(src, dst, cv2.RANSAC, ransacReprojThreshold=5.0)
    return H

@staticmethod
def apply_homography(px: float, py: float, H: np.ndarray) -> tuple:
    """Apply homography to a single point."""
    point  = np.array([[[px, py]]], dtype=np.float32)
    result = cv2.perspectiveTransform(point, H)
    return float(result[0][0][0]), float(result[0][0][1])  # pan, tilt

sections.heading: Laser Alignment Coverage Tips
sections.content: Point coverage across the frame is critical for alignment accuracy. A poorly distributed set of calibration points creates large errors in uncovered regions.
list: Minimum 12 points: Below this, the homography can be numerically unstable
Frame coverage: Always include at least one point near each corner of the frame
Avoid clustering: Spread points evenly — 10 clustered points in the center is worse than 12 spread across the frame
Edge points matter most: The system is most likely to point near the center, but calibration errors are largest at the edges
Re-alignment trigger: If the laser consistently misses targets by a fixed offset, run LASER ALIGNMENT again — do not rely solely on trim sliders for large corrections

sections.heading: MATLAB Stereo Calibration (Optional)
sections.content: If you have MATLAB with the Computer Vision Toolbox installed, you can use the MATLAB ENGINE tab for higher-precision calibration. MATLAB's Stereo Camera Calibrator App provides additional outlier rejection and visual diagnostics not available in the Python path.
sections.alertContent: MATLAB Calibration Workflow: (1) Run MATLAB Stereo Camera Calibrator App → Export to .mat file. (2) Go to DATA BRIDGE tab in NEXUS. (3) Load your .mat file → Convert → Saves as data/stereo_calibration_bridge.npz. (4) In Settings tab, set STEREO_FILE to the bridge file. (5) Restart engine to use MATLAB-precision calibration.

sections.heading: Recalibration Triggers
sections.content: You should recalibrate stereo whenever any of the following occur:
list: Cameras were physically moved relative to each other (mount loosened, dropped, adjusted)
Depth estimates appear consistently wrong by more than 10%
RMS error was > 1.0 px in a previous calibration
Camera lenses were refocused or replaced
You switched to a different camera pair

sections.heading: Recalibration Triggers (Laser Alignment)
sections.content: You should redo laser alignment whenever:
list: The pan/tilt mechanism was physically repositioned relative to the cameras
Servo endpoints were changed in the ESP32 firmware
The laser consistently aims to the wrong position even after trim correction
Trim sliders have reached their maximum (±18°) and still cannot correct the error
You replaced either servo or the entire pan/tilt assembly
